reports/physics_featurization/FINDINGS.m.text:
# Physics Featurization — Final Claims (critic-vetted)

Question (Jon): *"break all my data into physics so the ML is better."* This is the honest,
gated answer. Substrate research only — no advisory authority claimed. Every margin below is
average-precision (AP), held-out, with a clustered-by-station bootstrap CI and a within-station
label-permutation null (n=2000), Benjamini-Hochberg corrected across the whole 36-test family.
A null is a kept result.

Authoritative artifacts: `tokyo_hypoxia_pi_vs_raw/report.json` + `REPORT.md` (full 2010–2026 run,
N=22,096 station-days, 4 stations). Coverage map: `COVERAGE_MAP.md`.

## The two questions, separated

"Physics → better ML" is really **two** claims, and they get opposite verdicts:
- **(A) Use physics *measurements* as features** — does feeding the model raw physical
  quantities help?
- **(B) Re-encode them as dimensionless π groups** — does the *nondimensionalisation* help on
  top of that?

---

## Claim A — physics measurements predict hypoxia ONSET

**Label: CANDIDATE in-distribution; NULL for transfer.**

| Holdout | raw vs climatology | raw vs bottom-temp-only | verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| temporal (in-distribution) | **+0.110 AP**, CI [0.036, 0.138], perm✓, BH✓ | **+0.103 AP**, CI [0.090, 0.116] | SUPPORTED |
| LOSO (within-bay, shared-time) | +0.056, CI [0.021, 0.109] | +0.028, CI incl 0 | EXPLORATORY (confounded) |
| **blocked (station+time, honest transfer)** | +0.023, **CI [−0.017, 0.129] incl 0** | −0.032, CI incl 0 | **NOT SUPPORTED** |

- Real, multi-feature signal **in-distribution**: raw beats both a day-of-year climatology
  (+0.110) and the single best driver, antecedent bottom-water temperature (+0.103). So it is
  not a one-variable artifact within the training distribution.
- It **does not survive an honest station+time blocked holdout** (CI includes 0). The within-bay
  LOSO "+0.056" was a shared-temporal-forcing artifact (4 stations, same bay, same calendar
  days) — confirmed by the blocked test, exactly as the critic warned.
- This **reproduces and bounds** the already-banked `onset-law-replicates-tokyo-hypoxia`
  finding; it is not a new win. Anti-bloat: do not double-count it.
- Effect size (in-distribution): raw onset AP 0.443 vs base rate 0.070; ΔAP over climatology
  **+0.110**, CI [0.036, 0.138]. Limitation: exceedance screening, not calibrated probability;
  4 stations → clustered CI underpowered.

## Claim B — dimensionless π re-encoding improves ML

**Label: NULL (robust). This is the headline negative result.**

| comparison | temporal | LOSO | blocked |
|---|---|---|---|
| π vs raw (onset) | −0.186, CI excl 0 | −0.139, CI excl 0 | −0.151, CI excl 0 |
| π+raw vs raw (onset) | +0.007, CI incl 0 | +0.008, CI incl 0 | −0.007, CI incl 0 |
| π vs raw (state) | −0.044, CI excl 0 | −0.035, CI excl 0 | −0.068, CI incl 0 |

> Note: report.json's `ci_excludes_zero` flag is *one-sided* (fires only when a candidate
> BEATS its baseline). For π-vs-raw the margin is negative and the CI bounds are both negative
> (e.g. onset temporal [−0.218, −0.089]), so they exclude 0 on the *worse* side — π is
> significantly worse. The flag reads false there; the numeric bounds are what matter.

- π features alone are **far worse** than raw everywhere (CI excludes 0 in every onset cell).
- Adding π **on top of** raw adds essentially nothing — the only CI excluding 0 anywhere is
  +0.006 AP (state, temporal); negligible and not in the onset regime that matters.
- Mechanism (corrected after lit review, deep-research wf_e312f954): the null is NOT "trees are
  scale-invariant so π is useless" — that reasoning is wrong (a π-group is a nonlinear
  ratio/product a tree cannot build for free from axis-aligned splits, so a *good* π ratio could
  in principle help). The real reasons are (a) the **information ceiling** — IT-pi proves
  achievable error ≥ e^(−I), so nondimensionalising adds no predictive power beyond the
  information already in the variables (Yuan & Lozano-Durán, Nat. Commun. 2025); and (b) our
  hand-π set was **lossy/incomplete** (collapsing two temperatures into one contrast throws away
  information, and Buckingham-π needs the *complete* governing-variable set or the right group
  can't form). Raw measurements retain the full information; our π transform did not add any.
- Consistent with the lab's prior result that tensor/π *decompositions* wash under hardening.
  The lever is physics *as features*, not physics *as a dimensionless re-encoding*.

## Claim C — next-day state nowcast

**Label: CANDIDATE in-distribution; persistence-ceilinged under transfer.**

- Temporal: raw beats persistence +0.090 AP (CI [0.039, 0.145]).
- LOSO & blocked: **persistence (AP ~0.81–0.82) is unbeaten** by any physics model — next-day
  hypoxia is highly autocorrelated, so physics adds nothing out-of-distribution.

## Cross-system replication — CalCOFI deep oxygen (the test Tokyo couldn't do)

Tokyo's 4 stations share one bay and the same calendar forcing, so its "transfer" leaked.
**CalCOFI** has 74 modelled stations spanning the CA Current (modelled-subset lat ~26–38 N;
full grid wider; median nearest-neighbour 73.5 km), so leave-one-station-out is *genuine*
geographic transfer. Task (regression): predict
deep oxygen (175–250 m) from upper-column physics; metric = RMSE skill, clustered-by-station CI,
within-station permutation null (n=2000). Artifact: `calcofi_oxygen_transfer/report.json`
(N=9,625 casts). *(Critic round 2 BLOCKED the first version for a weak baseline; these are the
corrected, honest numbers.)*

**Claim D — physics measurements predict deep oxygen and DO transfer across systems. Label:
CANDIDATE (modest, mechanism = water-mass identity).**

| margin | temporal | LOSO (transfer) | blocked | reading |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| raw_phys vs season-clim (location-BLIND floor) | 0.368 | 0.472 | 0.372 | inflated — mostly between-station OMZ structure |
| raw_phys vs **station+season** (honest) | **0.091** | n/a* | n/a* | real but small |
| raw_phys vs **lat/lon** (physics *beyond* location) | 0.080 | **0.196** | 0.123 | the genuinely novel margin, clustered CI excl 0 (perm null is within-station only — see note) |
| deep-100 m-only vs surface-only | 0.297 | 0.364 | 0.306 | carrier is 100 m water-mass T/S, **not** surface stratification |
| raw (+nutrients) vs raw_phys | 0.093 | 0.075 | 0.096 | nutrient lift is Redfield/AOU coupling, not independent |

*station+season baseline is undefined for a held-out station (that's the point of transfer).

- A **real, cross-system-transferable** physics→oxygen signal exists (raw_phys beats a lat/lon
  location lookup by skill ~0.20 leave-one-station-out, clustered-by-station CI [0.077, 0.111]
  excludes 0) — this is the positive Tokyo structurally could not deliver. *Note:* the
  permutation null shuffles the target **within** station, so it validates the within-station
  signal only — it does **not** test the between-station (cross-system) magnitude; that rests on
  the lat/lon-baseline margin and the clustered CI above.
- But it is **modest and is water-mass identity, not "stratification forecasting"**: the carrier
  is the 100 m T/S adjacent to the target (oxygen along isopycnals / apparent oxygen
  utilisation), surface stratification adds little, and the big 0.37–0.47 numbers were against a
  location-blind baseline. Nutrient lift is stoichiometric (Redfield), not an independent driver.

**Claim B replicates here (π re-encoding NULL):** π is worse than raw in all three splits
(π-vs-raw skill −0.18 / −0.30 / −0.22, CI excl 0 on the negative side), and π+raw adds nothing
over raw (CI incl 0). The π-is-useless result now holds in **two independent domains**.

## The two domains reconcile

- **Tokyo (forecasting a future temporal transition — hypoxia onset):** physics helps
  in-distribution but **does NOT survive honest transfer** (driver-null reasserts).
- **CalCOFI (screening a contemporaneous spatial structure — deep oxygen):** physics **does
  transfer** across systems, because the water-mass T/S↔oxygen relationship is a stationary
  physical law that holds everywhere — but the effect is modest and is water-mass identity.
- **In BOTH, dimensionless-π re-encoding is dead.**

So "physics → better ML" transfers for *structural/spatial screening governed by a universal
relationship*, not for *forecasting future transitions* — and never via a nondimensional
re-encoding.

## Phase A — estate coverage (the substrate ceiling)

**Label: REFERENCE (map).** Of 222 sources / 5,191 variables, **49 sources can form ≥1
dimensionless group**; the rest are single-source analytes (e.g. mussel_watch compounds) or
metadata that cannot be nondimensionalised. "Break ALL data into physics" is therefore real for
a minority of richly-instrumented sources (Tokyo Bay, fixed platforms, OOI moorings, CEDEN
chem) and a no-op for the bulk. See `COVERAGE_MAP.md`.

---

## Bottom line for the program

1. **Physics-as-features** transfers **only for structural/spatial screening**, not for
   temporal forecasting. Tokyo onset: real in-distribution, **fails** honest station+time
   transfer. CalCOFI deep-oxygen: **transfers** across 74 stations, but modestly (skill ~0.20
   beyond a location lookup) and the mechanism is water-mass T/S identity, not stratification.
2. **Physics-as-dimensionless-π**: **dead in both domains** — never beats raw, adds nothing on
   top (CI incl 0). Stop investing in π/tensor re-encodings as a predictor (state-QA only, as
   already concluded). This is the durable, reusable negative.
3. **The honest lever** for "better ML from physics" is richer *physical measurement coverage*
   at new sites (so a model has real drivers to read), plus the onset reframe — not a
   nondimensional transform of the data we already have.

## Literature verdict — can a universal π-interlingua exist? (deep-research wf_e312f954)

Settled mathematics, 22 verified claims (primary/arXiv/peer-reviewed): **no.** (1) Substrate
poverty is a theorem — Buckingham-π needs every quantity dimensioned and ≥2 commensurable ones;
#groups = m − rank(D), so counts/categories/IDs/lone analytes form **zero** groups (our 49/222 is
a direct consequence). (2) A shared dimensionless coordinate is **non-unique with no importance
ordering** → not a predictive representation without extra data-driven selection. (3) **Information
ceiling** — IT-π proves achievable error ≥ e^(−I) for *any* model, so nondimensionalising adds no
predictive power beyond the variables' information (Yuan & Lozano-Durán, Nat. Commun. 2025). Where
dimensional structure *does* pay: physics-law recovery (AI-Feynman 15%→90%) and exact transfer
between *dimensionally-similar same-physics* systems (~480% simulated vs ~11% real) — never
arbitrary cross-domain. Our π-null is textbook.

## Foundation models as the learned alternative (deep-research wf_dda168cf)

24 verified claims: foundation models deliver **real but bounded** cross-dataset transfer — not
free/zero-shot/schema-agnostic. Tabular FMs (CARTE, TabSTAR, TabPFNv2, TabICL) beat tuned GBDTs
only in narrow regimes (small-data, text-rich/semantic column names, or in-context classification)
and still need per-task fine-tuning or in-context labels; trees still win on general/large/
regression/open-environment. CARTE is the one genuine keyless cross-table method but **leans on
meaningful column-name/string semantics** and is shown only on its authors' small-data benchmark.
Scientific FMs (Poseidon, Aurora) show real cross-*system* transfer beating strong baselines — but
via fine-tuning within **co-registered continuous-field grids**, NOT keyless/cross-schema; ClimaX
*degrades* under no-analog shift. **For a heterogeneous lakehouse with no shared keys, neither
plausibly gives free cross-source transfer**; the lab's own prior finding (CARTE/TabSTAR
alignment-validated, none beats persistence) reinforces low expectations. Cheapest local test if
ever pursued: a small-data, semantic-column CARTE/TabSTAR A/B vs tuned CatBoost on one text-rich
table — expect a tie/loss.

## IT-π information probe — turning the null into a tool (`it_pi_prescreen/`)

A model-free, permutation-calibrated **diagnostic**: estimate MI(features; target) (Ross/KSG kNN)
vs a shuffled-target null → does a feature set carry detectable signal *before* you model? Validated
(critic-approved): a synthetic **weak-but-real** effect reads SIGNAL (MI 0.017, perm p=0.04) — it
does **not** false-stop marginal escapes — while pure-noise/permuted targets read NO_SIGNAL; real
tasks (Tokyo onset, CalCOFI O₂) read SIGNAL with ceilings tracking achieved skill. It is a probe,
not a kill-switch: NO_SIGNAL = "no MI in *these* inputs," and an MI-significant-but-kNN-weak case is
flagged "info present, not extractable — try a stronger model," never "abandon."

## What this would be good for
An in-distribution bottom-hypoxia onset screen for Tokyo Bay's monitored stations (beats a
calendar climatology). NOT a cross-site/cold-start tool, and NOT evidence for π/tensor
featurisation. For a NULL it tells the lab to **stop building dimensionless-π feature layers**
and to treat physics value as measurement-coverage, in-distribution, onset-scoped.

## Reproduce
```
python research/physics_featurization/build_dimensional_registry.py
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES="" python research/physics_featurization/gate_tokyo_hypoxia.py
CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES="" python -m pytest tests/test_physics_featurization.py -q
```

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab
project_docs.text:
# Monterey Bay AI Lab

**A multi-hazard coastal warning lab for rare water-quality events — anchored on two decades of Monterey Bay mooring physics.**

The Monterey Bay AI Lab is a **multi-hazard coastal warning lab**. Its two current claimable pillars are **statewide bacterial-exceedance warning** and **domoic-acid / HAB next-visit warning**; both are event-forecasting problems where the model beats an honest operational baseline. The 21-year MBARI M1/M2 mooring record is the physical **backbone** that grounds and calibrates the work, not the forecasting headline.

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab
project_docs.text: ## Core Capabilities

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab
project_docs.text:
### 1. Statewide Event-Driven Forecasting — the headline
A multimodal engine predicting rare, non-persistent events (bacterial exceedances) across 800+ California stations.
- **Verified margin:** on a statewide California enterococcus benchmark (1.32M lab **assays** → ~442k modeled station-days, of which ~89k are in the deployable-stratum test split; test events ≥2022), a calibrated gradient-boosted model reaches **ROC-AUC 0.868 / AP 0.5099** on the **statewide deployable stratum** (California marine enterococcus in the standard rainfall/runoff regime) — the honest headline, since the cross-border San Diego/Tijuana plume is a 2022 data-regime artifact and is reported separately, not pooled. It beats the deployed AB411 rain rule (AP 0.18) and a Virtual-Beach-class MLR (AP 0.38), is well-calibrated after isotonic calibration (ECE 0.009 at the operational 2-day lab-result reveal lag — the AP/ECE include the confirmed harmonic spring-neap tidal-range escape, +0.011 AP over rain+discharge; see `signals/catalog.yaml` `tide_range_springneap` — the tracked, byte-reproducible `expected/` outputs; see `MODEL_SUITE.md`), and generalizes spatially: it beats both the regulatory rule and station-memory in **9/9 leave-one-county-out folds**. Decision-support-grade. (The older "AUC 0.888" headline was the artifact-inflated pooled/multi-analyte figure and has been retired — see `research/bacteria/reproduce/PAPER_DRAFT.md`, which derives the honest deployable stratum and the reveal-lag-enforced metrics above.)
- **The honest frontier:** site memory is a strong baseline (a recently-dirty beach tends to stay dirty; station-memory alone reaches AUC ~0.77 / AP 0.28 on the same statewide deployable stratum). The model already beats it meaningfully (+0.23 AP) with environmental drivers, and — verified — **generalizes to beaches it was never trained on**: under leave-one-beach-out cross-validation the driver model holds **AP 0.494 / ROC-AUC 0.852** on never-seen beaches, still beating station-memory (AP 0.28) and the AB411 rule (AP 0.18), so the skill is a transferable environmental pattern, not per-station memorization. The open frontier is extending risk estimates to the beaches we **don't** monitor. A learned spatial surface from *raw* station coordinates or a nearest-neighbor exceedance lag turns out to be a **wash** — no measurable lift (all spatial arms AP 0.497) because the per-station / county / statewide rate features already encode location — while residual per-beach autocorrelation remains (Moran's I ≈ 0.20, one-sided p<0.01). The obvious next lever — **physical spatial covariates** (outfall / river-mouth distance, embayment) — was then tested and is *also* a wash (it underperforms the lat/lon arm by −0.028 AP under leave-one-county-out and does not generalize), so there is currently **no demonstrated lever** that turns the residual clustering into transferable skill; it remains an open problem. These results reproduce from `research/bacteria/spatial_autocorr.py` (leave-one-beach-out + Moran's I), `research/bacteria/spatial_drivers_experiment.py` (the raw-coordinate wash), and `research/bacteria/physical_spatial_covariates.py` (the physical-covariate wash).

# Data Provenance & License

**The Apache-2.0 LICENSE covers the code only.** Third-party data retrieved or redistributed
by this project remains under its providers' terms. Verify each source's terms before
redistribution or commercial use.

project_docs.title: Data Provenance & License
project_docs.text:
## Sources

| Dataset | Provider | Access | Terms |
|---|---|---|---|
| M1 / M2 mooring time series | MBARI | OPeNDAP `https://dods.mbari.org/opendap/...` | MBARI data use terms |
| Sea-surface temperature (MUR SST) | NASA JPL PO.DAAC | NOAA MUR cache | NASA open data |
| Buoy waves/wind (NDBC 46042) | NOAA NDBC | `ndbc.noaa.gov` | U.S. Gov public domain |
| Tides / water level (CO-OPS) | NOAA CO-OPS | `tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov` | U.S. Gov public domain |
| Upwelling indices | NOAA | public feeds | U.S. Gov public domain |
| CA beach water-quality (BeachWatch) | CA SWRCB / CDPH | `waterqualitydata.us` / state records | CA public records |
| CalHABMAP pier domoic acid (particulate DA) | CalHABMAP / SCCOOS | ERDDAP `erddap.sccoos.org` | public research data (shipped: `data/external_curated/habmap_cdph/habmap_cdph.parquet`, 304 KB) |

project_docs.title: Data Provenance & License
project_docs.text:
## How the pipeline reads data

Code never hardcodes a path or cloud project. Configure via environment variables:

- `MBAL_PROJECT_ROOT` — repo / working root
- `MBAL_SOURCE_PARQUET` — curated source panel
- `MBAL_CACHE_DIR` — feature/driver cache (`nn_cache`)
- `MBAL_LAKEHOUSE_DIR` — lakehouse outputs (`lakehouse`)
- `MBAL_GCP_PROJECT` — your own GCP project, if pulling from BigQuery

project_docs.title: Model Suite - what exists, what has deps, what is claimable
project_docs.text:
# Model Suite - what exists, what has deps, what is claimable

Generated from `research/model_lab/model_registry.yaml` by `research/model_lab/model_suite.py`.
Fail-closed: a model is **claimable** only if the registry marks it so AND its evidence file exists.
If a dashboard, copied workspace, or report disagrees, the registry wins; see `docs/SOURCE_OF_TRUTH.md`.

- registry validation: PASS
- models registered: 20
- claimable now: 2

| model | family | task | neural? | deps available? | beats baseline? | calibrated? | claimable? | status | blocker |
|---|---|---|:-:|:-:|---|:-:|:-:|---|---|
| `bacteria_hgbt_isotonic` | tree | bacteria_classification | no | yes | historical retrospective win - beats AB411, Virtual-Beach MLR, and station-memory | yes | no | retired_claim | superseded by bacteria_catboost (2026-06-26). Retained only as a reproducibility reference; do not treat as the active production candidate. |
| `bacteria_hgbt_spatial` | tree | bacteria_classification | no | yes | no - raw coordinates and neighbour-lag are a wash (all spatial arms AP 0.497 = the no-spatial driver model); per-station/county/statewide rate features already encode location | yes | no | negative_result | raw-coordinate spatial surface does not beat the no-spatial driver model; physical covariates (outfall/river-mouth distance) were also tested and are a wash (-0.0018 AP vs lat/lon on holdout, -0.0283 AP under leave-one-county-out - do not generalize); no demonstrated lever for the residual per-beach clustering |
| `bacteria_xgboost` | tree | bacteria_classification | no | yes | comparator - run via the suite to test the data ceiling vs HGBT | yes | no | benchmark | promote only if it beats the best operational baseline AND calibrates (it won't materially beat HGBT - data ceiling) |
| `bacteria_catboost` | tree | bacteria_classification | no | yes | YES vs HGBT, paired-verified - suite AP 0.507 vs HGBT 0.4938 (dAP +0.0132), paired bootstrap CI [0.010, 0.0164] EXCLUDES 0, wins 13/15 leave-one-county-out, ECE 0.0095 vs 0.0174. Full-driver paired test (rain+discharge, same obs hash a54b3795): model_hgbt_calibrated AP=0.5297 vs frozen HGBT 0.5099. Champion-reproducibility gate: 10 PASS 2 WARN (2026-06-26). | yes | **YES** | production_candidate | prospective forward-time pilot (same blocker as HGBT); CEDEN data frozen at 2026-03-05 - forward scoring pending BigQuery auth restoration |
| `bacteria_lightgbm` | tree | bacteria_classification | no | yes | YES vs HGBT, paired-verified - AP 0.5019 vs 0.4938 (dAP +0.0081), paired bootstrap CI [0.0057, 0.0105] EXCLUDES 0, wins 14/15 leave-one-county-out. Real gate-clearing boost, smaller than CatBoost; second confirmation that the tree ceiling is ~0.50-0.51. | yes | no | benchmark | modest (+0.008 AP) and one fixed-seed split; CatBoost is the stronger upgrade candidate. Promotion still needs the freeze/reproduction gate. |
| `bacteria_stack` | tree | bacteria_classification | no | yes | comparator - the blend most likely to beat a single plateaued model; measured in the all-data suite rerun | yes | no | benchmark | promote only on a material, repeated lift over HGBT with deployable calibration |
| `bacteria_mlp_plr` | neural | bacteria_classification | yes | yes | comparator - fair modern deep-tabular test; measured in the all-data suite rerun | yes | no | benchmark | promote only if it materially beats HGBT AND calibrates (prior: an earlier plain entity-emb MLP tied HGBT) |
| `bacteria_hdc_vsa` | symbolic | bacteria_classification | no | yes | SHADOW PASS vs operational comparator only - full HDC/VSA gate found best bacteria AP 0.41238 vs Virtual-Beach MLR 0.37544 (dAP +0.03694, CI [0.02701, 0.04742], paired perm p=0.00498) on EXCLUDE_SAN_DIEGO; unified pi-panel was NULL and no product claim exists until paired against the frozen HGBT/CatBoost champion | yes | no | benchmark | shadow-only until paired directly against the frozen HGBT/CatBoost champion and hardened with adversarial target-source exclusion where applicable; current pass is operational-comparator evidence, not a deployed model upgrade |
| `bacteria_tabpfn_matched` | foundation | bacteria_classification | yes | yes | comparator only - answers 'did you try a tabular foundation model?' (NEW_LANGUAGES_TEST_PLAN item 63). Expected within noise of HGBT per the data ceiling; not a claim. | yes | no | benchmark | NOT a plain model_suite factory by design: the suite trains on the full ~270k-row pre-2020 set, but TabPFN caps at ~50k (v2 ~10k), so a bare drop-in would confound model class with train-set size. The matched-n harness fixes this (subsample both arms identically). Promote only if tabpfn_matched materially beats hgbt_matched at matched n AND calibration holds. |
| `xgboost_forecast_v2` | tree | m1_forecasting | no | yes | horizon-specific - gated by best-naive (ops/seasonal_naive.py); +72h tier rejected | no | no | benchmark | promotion is per target/horizon cell via release_gate/mbal_promotion_matrix.py |
| `air_temperature_sweep` | baseline | m1_forecasting | no | yes | comparator only -- sweeps xgboost/chronos/NeuralForecast engines on air_temperature vs persistence/seasonal-naive with null controls | no | no | benchm

The earlier 'beats C-HARM 8/8, ~10x' framing is a BROKEN-BASELINE MIRAGE (C-HARM is sub-random here, ROC-AUC 0.30) - retained only as the operational-incumbent comparison. (These are the sweep's own internal incumbent run, NOT the da_forecast.json headline AP 0.2034 -- do not cross-compare across runs; the gate decision is internal to this sweep.) Kept as the honest 'more data did not help' ablation, not a promotion. |
| `charm_da_nowcast` | baseline | domoic_acid_forecasting | no | yes | n/a - operational incumbent. Our prior-visit forecast out-scores it 8/8, but C-HARM is sub-random on this overlap (ROC-AUC 0.30) so that win is a broken-baseline mirage; plain persistence also beats it 6.68x. project_docs.title: Model Suite - what exists, what has deps, what is claimable
project_docs.text:
## Legend
- **neural?** - `neural`/`foundation`/`continual` families are neural; `tree`/`baseline` are not. (The `continual` family is reserved and currently has no registered model: the dense mixture-of-experts design once kept here was retired as bloat, and what survives is a compact 2,772,504-param Conv1d+MLP forecaster in `sota_continual_learning/`, not registered as a claimable model.)
- **deps available?** - whether the model's declared dependencies import in this environment. This is not an execution smoke test for the registry entrypoint.
- **beats baseline?** - against the honest baseline (best-naive for forecasting; AB411 / Virtual-Beach MLR / station-memory for bacteria), stated straight.
- **claimable?** - safe to put in a pitch/paper. Only `production_candidate` (and, case-by-case, a promoted `benchmark`) with on-disk evidence qualifies.

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Readiness Register
project_docs.text:
# Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Readiness Register

Last verified: 2026-06-13

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Readiness Register
project_docs.text:
## Decision

Status: **local production gate passed; senior-ready gate is held on true external-smoke evidence and current high-risk worktree state.**

The project is now shaped as a production candidate: source control is initialized,
generated artifacts are ignored, unit tests pass, release-gate tooling exists, and the
portable CPU smoke job succeeded from `C:\mbal_portable_smoke`. It is not approved for
external deployment, global SOTA claims, or broad driver-value claims. Promotion remains
target/horizon-specific, and the senior gate must remain below `PASS` until an external
runner writes `release_gate/reports/external_smoke_report.json` with runner-owned storage
paths and the in-flight worktree is reduced below the senior-gate risk threshold.

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Readiness Register
project_docs.text:
## Verified Local State

- Editable install: `python -m pip install -e .[test]` succeeded.
- Test suite: `python ops\run_tests.py` passes in the current local verification.
- Release gate: `python release_gate\mbal_release_gate.py --project-root . --output-dir release_gate\reports` returns `PASS`.
- Daily learnings: `python ops\generate_daily_learnings.py --project-root .` writes `archive\reports\MBAL_AI_DAILY_LEARNINGS.md`.
- Senior gate: `python release_gate\mbal_sr_manager_gate.py --project-root . --output release_gate\reports\sr_manager_gate_report.json --no-exit-code` returns `WARN` until `external_smoke_report.json` exists and passes, and until the git worktree has fewer than 20 changed/untracked paths.
- GPU stack: RTX 5090 CUDA/Torch/XGBoost checks pass in the release gate.
- Curated dataset: validation report matches 3,807,370 expected rows across 351 Parquet files.
- Promotion matrix: 5,566 rows with 39 `promote`, 12 `candidate_split_mismatch`, 877 `reject`, and 4,638 `insufficient_data`; the 39 promoted rows collapse to 12 unique target/horizon cells and 34 unique model cells.
- Portable smoke: `python .\mbal_train.py --jobs .\ops\jobs.smoke.json --accel cpu --no-wake-lock --no-gpu-preflight --force` passed from `C:\mbal_portable_smoke\src` with `scored_rows=4`, `summary.json`, `leaderboard.csv`, `cv_predictions.parquet`, and lakehouse artifacts.
- Artifact audit: smoke outputs contain no absolute project/data paths from the local working tree. The raw process log contains the local Python installation path from a third-party warning; this is runtime-environment noise, not a project artifact dependency.
- **Hardware Safety**: Training is protected by `ops/mbal_sota_utils.py:SafetyMonitor`, enforcing an 80% VRAM ceiling, 72h runtime limit, and 30m idle shutdown.

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Lakehouse Contracts
project_docs.text:
# Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Lakehouse Contracts

Generated: 2026-06-07

This project uses a production-grade local lakehouse architecture. Neural runs emit
structured artifacts under `lakehouse/` ensuring the workflow remains portable to
generic remote compute environments (Spark, Kubernetes, object storage, or managed
runtime platforms) without changing the core modeling contract.

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Lakehouse Contracts
project_docs.text:
## Layers

- `lakehouse/silver/forecast_splits/`
  Canonical split contracts. Each `split_id` records the expanding-window cutoffs,
  train window bounds, evaluated horizons, source fingerprint, and run configuration.

- `lakehouse/gold/forecast_runs/`
  Run manifests. Each `run_id` records model, loss, driver configuration, source
  fingerprint, cache version, environment knobs, and artifact paths.

- `lakehouse/gold/forecast_metrics/`
  Normalized target/horizon metrics keyed by `run_id`, `split_id`, `unique_id`,
  and `horizon_h`. Carries the honest-baseline columns `seasonal_naive_rmse` and
  `skill_vs_best_naive_pct` (skill vs the better of persistence and seasonal-naive),
  backfilled by `ops/backfill_seasonal_naive.py` from the prediction partitions.

- `lakehouse/gold/forecast_predictions/`
  Per-run prediction partitions keyed by `run_id`. These preserve the original
  `cv_predictions.parquet` shape while adding `run_id`, `split_id`, and prediction
  column metadata.

project_docs.title: Monterey Bay AI Lab Production Lakehouse Contracts
project_docs.text:
## Architectural Tier Mapping

- **Bronze (Raw/Ingest):**
  Immutable copies of raw MBARI source data and external driver inputs.

- **Silver (Curated/Enriched):**
  QC-applied hourly matrix, observed mask, driver availability table, and
  `forecast_splits`. These are ready for modeling and feature engineering.

- **Gold (Aggregated/Insights):**
  `forecast_runs`, `forecast_metrics`, `forecast_predictions`, and final comparison
  leaderboards. These are consumer-ready artifacts for scientists and managers.

project_docs.title: Cyano Onset Claim-Card Red-Team
project_docs.text:
# Cyano Onset Claim-Card Red-Team

**Verdict:** CLAIMABLE_BUT_SCOPED

Latitude/quadratic geography is a real cold-start cyano-onset law under held-out state, lat-band, and year tests; do not sell the weak photoperiod-only mechanism, and treat winter met as a secondary hardening path.

project_docs.title: Cyano Onset Claim-Card Red-Team
project_docs.text:
## Hard Checks
- Original leave-one-state lat-quad skill: 0.2018 CI [0.0442, 0.3151]
- Excluding 2026 leave-one-state lat-quad skill: 0.2005 CI [0.0426, 0.2978]
- Prior-winter met join rows: 11,240
- Met materially adds to lat-quad: True

project_docs.title: Cyano Onset Claim-Card Red-Team
project_docs.text:
## Claim Discipline
- Claim onset timing, not abundance.
- Anchor on lat/quadratic held-out skill, not photoperiod-only.
- Use as a sampling-start prior for cold-start lakes.

project_docs.title: Domoic-acid forecast -- next-visit pDA >= 0.5 ng/mL (=500 ng/L), ~1-week lead
project_docs.text:
# Domoic-acid forecast -- next-visit pDA >= 0.5 ng/mL (=500 ng/L), ~1-week lead

Train <= 2018 (n=3209, 148 events) | calib 2019-2020 | test > 2020 (n=1837, 52 events, base 0.028).

project_docs.title: Domoic-acid forecast -- next-visit pDA >= 0.5 ng/mL (=500 ng/L), ~1-week lead
project_docs.text:
## PRIMARY UNIT -- toxic ONSET on prior-clean cells (exc_prev==0)

The decision-relevant unit: forecasting a toxic event when the prior visit was CLEAN. Persistence is DEGENERATE here (exc_prev==0 => p=0 on every row), so the fair baseline is max(seasonal-naive, station-memory) -- NEVER persistence.

- model AP **0.1347** vs strongest non-degenerate baseline `seasonal_naive` AP 0.0259 => **margin +0.1089**
- station-clustered bootstrap CI95 [0.0566, 0.2078] (excludes 0, 100% boot+); perm-null p=0.002; N=1785, 34 onset events
- **Verdict: CI-SEPARATED POSITIVE**

project_docs.title: Domoic-acid forecast -- next-visit pDA >= 0.5 ng/mL (=500 ng/L), ~1-week lead
project_docs.text:
## Pooled time-held-out test (SECONDARY context -- AP, base rate ~3%)

Pooled AP mixes the trivial prior-exceed carry-over with onset; the strongest pooled baseline is persistence, so the pooled margin understates the onset skill above.

Feature-group ablation: the physical/nutrient **drivers HURT** (AP drops vs the lean set) -- so the headline model is `DA+precursor`, not the full driver matrix. Same driver-null pattern as M1 / news / wave-tide.

| method | AP | ROC-AUC | AP lift vs base |
|---|--:|--:|--:|
| seasonal_naive | 0.0369 | 0.6072 | 0.8 |
| persistence | 0.1383 | 0.6636 | 3.0 |
| station_memory | 0.0331 | 0.5499 | 0.72 |
| model_DA_history | 0.2133 | 0.8642 | 4.62 |
| model_DA+precursor (headline) | 0.2034 | 0.8421 | 4.41 |
| model_DA+precursor+drivers | 0.1634 | 0.8302 | 3.54 |

**Pooled verdict:** BEATS baselines -- pooled model AP 0.2034 vs best pooled baseline 0.1383 (persistence; see PRIMARY onset unit above for the headline).

**Leave-one-station-out:** model beats seasonal-naive in 8/8 stations.

| station | n | events | model AP | seasonal AP | beats |
|---|--:|--:|--:|--:|---|
| MontereyWharf | 275 | 49 | 0.5664 | 0.2437 | Y |
| TrinidadPier | 107 | 10 | 0.4222 | 0.0969 | Y |
| StearnsWharf | 883 | 29 | 0.2106 | 0.075 | Y |

# All-Silver Unsupervised Neural Critic

Verdict: PROMOTE APPROVED SCOPE TO RUN, NOT NAIVE PHYSICAL ALL-ROWS.

Accepted run: manifest-driven denoising autoencoder over all eligible lakehouse silver signal files.
Rejected run: every physical silver parquet row including duplicated normalized/source-native copies, catalog metadata, model outputs, row-enriched embeddings, forecast outputs, and labelish/advisory paths.

Why accepted: additive research-only artifacts; source data untouched; label-like columns dropped; normalized tables preferred over duplicate source-native copies; outputs/catalog/model embeddings excluded; permutation probes configured for diagnostics.

Current scope:
- files_scanned: 5,676
- eligible_files: 332
- excluded_files: 5,344
- scanned_rows: 6,451,532,509
- eligible_rows: 1,826,243,822
- eligible_sources: 255
- eligible_bytes: 19,269,874,703

Exclusions:
- catalog_or_inventory_metadata: 12
- derived_candidate_output: 3
- duplicate_external_curated_normalized_preferred: 59
- forecast_or_promotion_output: 251
- gold_or_discovery_output: 9
- labelish_path_diagnostic_only: 4
- model_embedding_or_translation_output: 3
- row_enriched_duplicate: 5,003

Risk controls:
- No lakehouse/gold writes; outputs stay under this run directory.
- Dedupe prefers normalized observations over matching external_curated sources.
- `--drop-labelish-columns` prevents obvious label leakage into AE training.
- Label readouts are diagnostic only and require permutation-null interpretation.
- Full pass is expensive: previous observed throughput around 19.5k rows/s implies roughly 26 hours for 1.826B rows, plus artifact/probe time.

project_docs.title: Physics Featurization — Final Claims (critic-vetted)
project_docs.text:
# Physics Featurization — Final Claims (critic-vetted)

Question (Jon): *"break all my data into physics so the ML is better."* This is the honest,
gated answer. Substrate research only — no advisory authority claimed. Every margin below is
average-precision (AP), held-out, with a clustered-by-station bootstrap CI and a within-station
label-permutation null (n=2000), Benjamini-Hochberg corrected across the whole 36-test family.
A null is a kept result.

Authoritative artifacts: `tokyo_hypoxia_pi_vs_raw/report.json` + `REPORT.md` (full 2010–2026 run,
N=22,096 station-days, 4 stations). Coverage map: `COVERAGE_MAP.md`.

project_docs.title: Physics Featurization — Final Claims (critic-vetted)
project_docs.text:
## The two questions, separated

"Physics → better ML" is really **two** claims, and they get opposite verdicts:
- **(A) Use physics *measurements* as features** — does feeding the model raw physical
  quantities help?
- **(B) Re-encode them as dimensionless π groups** — does the *nondimensionalisation* help on
  top of that?

---

project_docs.title: Physics Featurization — Final Claims (critic-vetted)
project_docs.text:
## Claim A — physics measurements predict hypoxia ONSET

**Label: CANDIDATE in-distribution; NULL for transfer.**

| Holdout | raw vs climatology | raw vs bottom-temp-only | verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| temporal (in-distribution) | **+0.110 AP**, CI [0.036, 0.138], perm✓, BH✓ | **+0.103 AP**, CI [0.090, 0.116] | SUPPORTED |
| LOSO (within-bay, shared-time) | +0.056, CI [0.021, 0.109] | +0.028, CI incl 0 | EXPLORATORY (confounded) |
| **blocked (station+time, honest transfer)** | +0.023, **CI [−0.017, 0.129] incl 0** | −0.032, CI incl 0 | **NOT SUPPORTED** |

- Real, multi-feature signal **in-distribution**: raw beats both a day-of-year climatology
  (+0.110) and the single best driver, antecedent bottom-water temperature (+0.103). So it is
  not a one-variable artifact within the training distribution.
- It **does not survive an honest station+time blocked holdout** (CI includes 0). The within-bay
  LOSO "+0.056" was a shared-temporal-forcing artifact (4 stations, same bay, same calendar
  days) — confirmed by the blocked test, exactly as the critic warned.
- This **reproduces and bounds** the already-banked `onset-law-replicates-tokyo-hypoxia`
  finding; it is not a new win. Anti-bloat: do not double-count it.
- Effect size (in-distribution): raw onset AP 0.443 vs base rate 0.070; ΔAP over climatology
  **+0.110**, CI [0.036, 0.138]. Limitation: exceedance screening, not calibrated probability;
  4 stations → clustered CI underpowered.

project_docs.title: MBAI Remote Research & Data Access Contract
project_docs.text:
# MBAI Remote Research & Data Access Contract

This document defines how the Monterey Bay AI Lab (MBAI) handles scaling normalized signals while protecting local compute resources and managing Google Cloud costs.

project_docs.title: MBAI Remote Research & Data Access Contract
project_docs.text:
## 1. The "Forge" vs. The "Evidence Layer"

We operate on a **Push-Only Architecture**.

project_docs.title: MBAI Remote Research & Data Access Contract
project_docs.text:
### The MBAI Forge (User's Machine / RTX 5090)
- **Role:** High-Performance Training (HPT), data normalization, and heuristic fusion.
- **Security:** Private and isolated. **No inbound traffic allowed.**
- **Workflow:** 
  1. Ingest raw data from Google BigQuery (one-time or scheduled sync).
  2. Perform multivariate regression and A/B testing locally.
  3. Emit normalized **Gold Artifacts** (Parquet files).
  4. **Push** Gold Artifacts to the Google Cloud Evidence Layer.

project_docs.title: Value Gate — is this worth building, or is it bloat?
project_docs.text:
# Value Gate — is this worth building, or is it bloat?

Binding for all agents (Codex, Claude, Gemini, Qwen). Run this gate **before you
implement or materially scope** any new feature, module, abstraction, driver, data source, model,
metric, doc, or "phase." Read-only audits, direct user-requested small fixes, bug triage, and
correctness/security repairs may proceed, but still report the evidence path. Its job: keep the project continually improving **without**
accreting bloat. Cite the result with a **falsifiable payload** — a bare "beats baseline"
is itself a gate failure (it's how cargo-cult citation gets caught):
*"Value gate: DO_NOW — beats <baseline> by <Δmetric>, test <path::name>"* or
*"rejected: <kill-category> — <one-line reason>"*.

This gate is itself held to its own bar: it is one short checklist, not a manifesto.

project_docs.title: Value Gate — is this worth building, or is it bloat?
project_docs.text:
## The 30-second gate — all five must be YES to build

1. **Beats a real baseline / answers a real question.** Does it measurably improve a
   result against an *honest* baseline (seasonal-naive, persistence, AB411, prior-lab,
   station-memory, the current metric) — or answer a question a stakeholder actually
   asked? "It's more sophisticated / more SOTA / more general" is **not** a yes.
2. **Additive & reversible — OR a correctness fix.** New *behavior* lands behind a
   default-off flag or as a new file and is removable cleanly (Delta phases, shadow
   dual-write off-by-default: yes; an in-place rewrite of the gold layer for a new
   feature: no). **Correctness-fix callout: reversibility is waived for fixes to
   leakage, dedup keys, median-not-mean, causality, and similar correctness bugs.**
   These fixes may be in-place and one-way, and fixing them is mandatory regardless of
   reversibility (see the data-correctness invariants in `AGENTS.md`).
3. **Testable, and you will test it.** Is there a concrete pass/fail test or a
   reproducible number you will add in the same change? If you can't test it, you can't
   claim it.
4. **Valuable NOW, not speculative.** Its value must be demonstrable now, not unprovable
   until some future step lands. Sequencing is fine — building a holdout harness *before*
   the validation it enables passes; building a feature whose worth can't be shown until
   a burn-in/dataset/evidence step that hasn't happened does not. If a named, scheduled
   task calls this in <= 2 steps, it's enabling; otherwise it's premature. When in doubt,
   do the cheaper enabling step first.
5. **Genuinely new, and improves a result we actually claim.** Not redundant with an
   existing path, and not *polishing* a case we deliberately **exclude** (e.g. tuning San
   Diego's calibration) or a stratum we don't deploy. **Carve-out:** work that would
   *change* the exclusion decision — expand coverage, or fix the reason a region was
   excluded — is a baseline-beating feature and PASSES.

If any answer is NO → **do not build the full thing.** State which kill-category it is, then run the
cheapest validation/probe if that is what would decide value, or report the task as blocked.

project_docs.title: Value Gate — is this worth building, or is it bloat?
project_docs.text:
## Kill list — name the reason when you reject something

- **Bloat** — needless complexity/abstraction; recovers what a simpler thing already gives.
- **Vapor** — scaffolding not wired to real work; a "framework" with no caller.
- **Premature** — depends on a burn-in / evidence / data step not yet done.
- **Ops-toggle** — a config/env flip or one-line invocation, not an engineering deliverable.
- **Redundant** — an existing path already covers it.
- **Cosmetic** — renames, reformatting, micro-metrics that change no decision.
- **Excluded-case polish** — optimizes a region/stratum/path we deliberately set aside
  *without changing the exclusion decision* (tuning excluded San Diego's calibration).
  NB: work that makes an excluded case *includable* is NOT this — it passes (see Q5).

project_docs.title: Open-Source Readiness
project_docs.text:
# Open-Source Readiness

This checklist defines the minimum bar before publishing the repository publicly.

project_docs.title: Open-Source Readiness
project_docs.text:
## Required

- `README.md` explains the project, setup, tests, Docker workflow, and data boundaries.
- `LICENSE` grants explicit open-source rights.
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` explains branch, test, and pull request expectations.
- `SECURITY.md` gives a private vulnerability reporting path.
- `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md` sets collaboration expectations.
- `pyproject.toml` declares package metadata and dependency groups.
- `Dockerfile` and `.dockerignore` support clean reproducibility checks.
- Tests pass with `python ops/run_tests.py`.

project_docs.title: Open-Source Readiness
project_docs.text:
## Data Boundary

Do not publish private credentials, local `.env` files, raw protected datasets, generated caches, or workstation-only artifacts. Public releases should include one of the following:

- Small synthetic fixtures.
- Publicly licensed sample data.
- Scripts that download public data from documented sources.
- Precomputed public artifacts with provenance and version notes.

project_docs.title: MBAL Public-Repo Publish Runbook
project_docs.text:
# MBAL Public-Repo Publish Runbook

**Status:** prep-complete; the destructive history purge is **NOT** run by this runbook
automatically — it is gated behind explicit owner approval (see Step 4).

This runbook is the **executable, fail-closed** sequence for cutting the public open-source
release of the Monterey Bay AI Lab repo. It exists because the working repo carries things a
public repo must never have:

- ~3.9 GB of raw third-party data (the 50 `data/external_raw/dpr_pur/pur19xx.zip` PUR zips,
  16 of them >100 MB — these also block `git push` to GitHub entirely),
- committed `*.xlsx` workbooks and other large/binary blobs in **history**,
- a personal contact email and private GCP identifiers (project `monterey-bay-lab`, the two
  GCS buckets, the BigQuery dataset) hardcoded in source,
- third-party collaborator PII under `docs/collab/` (untracked today, but must be kept out).

The non-negotiable rule: **nothing leaves this tree until `ops/prepublish_gate.py` prints
`RESULT: PASS`.** The gate is fail-closed (any blocker → exit 1).

---

project_docs.title: MBAL Public-Repo Publish Runbook
project_docs.text:
## Canonical ignore-set

`release/publish/gitignore.public` is the **canonical** public ignore-set and a **strict
superset** of the repo-root `.gitignore`. Step 2 copies it over the public tree as
`.gitignore`. `ops/prepublish_gate.py` Check 4 enforces the superset invariant — if you add a
rule to root `.gitignore`, mirror it into `gitignore.public` or the gate fails.

---

project_docs.title: MBAL Public-Repo Publish Runbook
project_docs.text:
## Step 0 — Scrub source (REL-04) and re-run until green

These are non-destructive content fixes on the working tree.

1. **Personal email → env var.** Done for the 5 fetcher/adapter files
   (`ops/find_open_datasets.py`, `ops/fetch_research_corpus.py`,
   `research/whale/global_mortality.py`, `research/whale/fetch_global_ocean.py`,
   `ops/data_fetch/adapters/ais_marinecadastre.py`): each now reads
   `os.environ.get("MBAL_CONTACT_EMAIL", "contact@example.org")`. Set `MBAL_CONTACT_EMAIL`
   in the environment for real runs.
2. **Remaining email / GCP literals.** The gate will list every *other* tracked file that
   still contains a personal email or a private GCP literal (`monterey-bay-lab`,
   `monterey-bay-lab-lakehouse`, `monterey-bay-lab-archive`, `blue_current_core_v2`). Those
   files are owned by OTHER remediation lanes — they must be scrubbed by their owners (e.g.
   `ops/cloud_sync.py`, `ops/run_cloud_sync.ps1`, `ops/run_daily_nowcast.ps1`,
   `research/bacteria/*`, `baylens/src/llm_client.py`, `TRAINING_DATA_VIZ.html`,
   `docs/collab/LAKEHOUSE_READONLY_ACCESS.md`). Do not publish until they are clean.

```bash
python ops/prepublish_gate.py --json release/publish/prepublish_report.json
```

Re-run after each scrub. **Do not proceed while any BLOCKER remains** other than the
big-blob/denied-path blockers that Step 4's purge is designed to remove.

---

project_docs.text:
# BEATING DEPLOYED PRACTICE IN BEACH-WATER QUALITY NOWCASTING: A LEAKAGE-CLEAN, CALIBRATED, STATEWIDE BENCHMARK ON THE CALIFORNIA MARINE ENTEROCOCCUS STANDARD

**Author:** Jon Dinneen
**Affiliation:** [to be completed]
**Correspondence:** [email]
**Draft for submission — not peer reviewed. Prepared 2026-06-09.**

> Reproduction materials for every number in this draft are in the same directory:
> see `REPRODUCE.md`. The headline configuration is
> `--label enterococcus --reveal-lag-days 2`; expected outputs are in `expected/`.

Suggested venues (any one; this is written venue-neutral as a short paper / extended abstract):
NeurIPS / ICLR "Tackling Climate Change with ML" workshop; ACM COMPASS; Environmental
Modelling & Software; Water Research; AGU Ocean Sciences; KDD Applied Data Science track.

---

project_docs.title: BEATING DEPLOYED PRACTICE IN BEACH-WATER QUALITY NOWCASTING: A LEAKAGE-CLEAN, CALIBRATED, STATEWIDE BENCHMARK ON THE CAL
project_docs.text:
## ABSTRACT

Beach advisories for fecal-indicator-bacteria (FIB) exceedance are issued from laboratory
cultures that return roughly 18-24 hours after a water sample is collected, so the same-day
public-health decision is effectively unguided or relies on a coarse wet-weather rule
(California AB411). We build a statewide California benchmark (1.32M assays, 802 monitoring
stations, 17 counties, 2005-2026) for NOWCASTING single-sample marine enterococcus
exceedance (>104 MPN/100 mL) using only information available at sampling time. With strictly
causal features — prior laboratory results enforced against the true lab-reveal lag, station
memory, seasonality, gridded rainfall, and USGS river first-flush discharge — a gradient-
boosted decision-tree model (with a harmonic spring-neap tidal-range feature), isotonically
calibrated, attains Average Precision (AP) 0.510 /
AUROC 0.868 on the honest statewide stratum. It beats, on the same data: the AB411 rain rule
(AP 0.178; ~2.8x), a Virtual-Beach-class per-site multiple logistic regression representing
DEPLOYED practice (AP 0.375; +0.12 AP, ~32%), and a station-memory baseline (AP 0.278) —
while remaining deploy-ready in calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE, 0.020). Under
leave-one-county-out (LOCO) spatial cross-validation, the model beats AB411 and station
memory in 9/9 held-out counties. We report negative and null results honestly: a 2022
San Diego / Tijuana-River sewage REGIME BREAK inflates pooled metrics and is excluded from
the headline; river discharge is largely rain-redundant; and a static probability calibrator
degrades under regime shift, which a prequential (online) recalibrator only partially repairs.
We release a leakage-controlled, reproducible benchmark and argue that the field's
publishable contribution is not a higher pooled AUC but a calibrated model that beats deployed
practice under honest, stratified, spatially-held-out evaluation.

---

project_docs.title: BEATING DEPLOYED PRACTICE IN BEACH-WATER QUALITY NOWCASTING: A LEAKAGE-CLEAN, CALIBRATED, STATEWIDE BENCHMARK ON THE CAL
project_docs.text:
## 1. INTRODUCTION

Recreational coastal waters are monitored for fecal-indicator bacteria; when a single-sample
standard is exceeded, an advisory or closure is posted. The operational bottleneck is time:
the culture-based laboratory assay returns ~18-24 hours after sampling, so on the day a
beachgoer is exposed, the manager either has yesterday's result or, under California's AB411
program, a wet-weather rule that posts an advisory when rainfall exceeds a threshold. AB411 is
known to be weak; the predictive tool actually deployed at many modern beach programs is EPA
Virtual Beach, a per-site multiple-linear/logistic regression on local hydrometeorological
drivers, or a USGS site-specific nowcast.

A large body of work reports machine-learning models for FIB nowcasting, often with
impressive pooled AUC. Two failure modes recur and motivate this paper. First, LEAKAGE: many
"prior result" features are constructed by sample index (the previous SAMPLE), not by reveal
time (the previous RESULT that had physically returned by prediction time); and spatially
pooled features can leak a held-out region into training. Second, ARTIFACTS: a model evaluated
across a measurement/program discontinuity can score the discontinuity rather than generalizable
skill, inflating a headline number that does not survive domain scrutiny.

Our contribution is an honest benchmark. We (i) define a single, standard-aligned target — the
AB411 marine enterococcus single-sample standard; (ii) construct strictly causal features with
an enforced lab-reveal lag; (iii) evaluate against a baseline ladder that includes a
Virtual-Beach-class model representing deployed practice, not only the weak AB411 rule;
(iv) stratify by region and era to surface, rather than hide, a regime break; (v) test spatial
generalization by leave-one-county-out; and (vi) treat calibration as an explicit deploy gate.
We find a model that beats deployed practice and generalizes — and we report where it does not.

---
